whatThisIs: A reference table of oral bioavailability and the conventional IV-to-PO dose ratio for the antibiotics that most often get switched at the bedside - the highly bioavailable ones (levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, moxifloxacin, linezolid, metronidazole, fluconazole, doxycycline, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, clindamycin) where the oral dose equals the IV dose, and the lower-bioavailability agents where the oral dose differs. Sophie Well shows the bioavailability percentage, the standard ratio, and a one-line note on when the switch is appropriate.
whenToUse: Use this on the morning rounds switch decision: a patient who is hemodynamically stable, afebrile, tolerating diet, with a functioning GI tract and source control achieved is a candidate for IV-to-PO conversion. Bioavailability is necessary but not sufficient - bacteremia depth, endovascular involvement, CNS penetration, drug-drug interactions (warfarin with TMP-SMX, antacids with fluoroquinolones), and the patient's own absorption (post-bariatric, short gut, ileus, severe gastritis) all matter. The table is a starting point; the antimicrobial stewardship team and the institutional formulary remain the authority for the specific agent and dose.
